name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ ٱتَّقُوا۟ رَبَّكُمْ ۚ إِنَّ زَلْزَلَةَ ٱلسَّاعَةِ شَىْءٌ عَظِيمٌ (١)
```

```english
O mankind! Be wary of your Lord! Indeed the quake of the Hour is a terrible thing. (1)
```

```arabic
يَوْمَ تَرَوْنَهَا تَذْهَلُ كُلُّ مُرْضِعَةٍ عَمَّآ أَرْضَعَتْ وَتَضَعُ كُلُّ ذَاتِ حَمْلٍ حَمْلَهَا وَتَرَى ٱلنَّاسَ سُكَـٰرَىٰ وَمَا هُم بِسُكَـٰرَىٰ وَلَـٰكِنَّ عَذَابَ ٱللَّهِ شَدِيدٌ (٢)
```

```english
The day that you will see it, every suckling female will be unmindful what she suckled, and every pregnant female will deliver her burden, and you will see the people drunk, yet they will not be drunken, but Allah’s punishment is severe. (2)
```

## (Commentary)

The Surah addresses the polytheists regarding the principles of religion (أصول الدين) by way of warning and intimidation, much like they were addressed in the Surahs revealed before the Migration (الهجرة). The context indicates that they still possessed influence and power. Simultaneously, it addresses the believers regarding matters such as prayer (الصلاة), the rituals of pilgrimage (الحج), performing good deeds, and the permission for fighting and struggle (الجهاد). This occurs in a context testifying that they had a newly formed society, standing on its own feet, and not lacking in numbers, preparation, or strength.

Accordingly, it is determined that the Surah is Medinan (مدنية), revealed in Medina between the Migration of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and the Battle of Badr. Its objective is to provide a detailed explanation of the principles of religion (أصول الدين) for the benefit of both the polytheist and the monotheist, and a general explanation of its branches (الفروع) for the benefit of the monotheists among the believers. This is because the details of the secondary legal rulings (الأحكام الفرعية) had not yet been legislated at that time, except for matters like prayer and pilgrimage, as mentioned in the Surah.

Because the invitation of the polytheists to the principles was through warning, and similarly, the exhortation of the believers toward the generalities of the branches was through the language of commanding godwariness (التقوى), the discourse expanded on the description of the Day of Resurrection. The Surah opens with the earthquake (الزلزلة), which is among its portents (أشراطها) and the cause of the earth's destruction and the crumbling of the mountains.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ اتَّقُوا رَبَّكُمْ إِنَّ زَلْزَلَةَ السَّاعَةِ شَيْءٌ عَظِيمٌ** — **"O mankind! Be wary of your Lord! Indeed the earthquake of the Hour is a terrible thing." (22:1)** The earthquake (الزلزلة) and the shaking (الزلزال) refer to the intensity of movement in a terrifying state. It seems to be derived through major derivation (الاشتقاق الكبير) from the root *zalla* (زل), meaning to slip; thus, it was repeated for emphasis and to indicate the recurrence of the slipping. This is common in its counterparts, such as *dhabdhaba* (ذبذب), *damdama* (دمدم), *kabkaba* (كبكب), *dakdaka* (دكدك), *rafrafa* (رفرف), and others.

The address encompasses all people—believer and disbeliever, male and female, those present and those absent, those currently existing and those yet to be born. This is achieved by making some of those present a medium for addressing the whole, due to the unity of all in their human species (النوع).

It is a command to the people to be wary of their Lord (اتقوا ربكم). The disbeliever practices this godwariness (التقوى) — *the state of being conscious of Allah's presence and guarding oneself against His displeasure* — through faith (الإيمان), while the believer does so by avoiding the violation of His commands and prohibitions in the branches of law (الفروع). The command is justified by the magnitude of the earthquake of the Hour; thus, it is an invitation by way of warning (الإنذار).

The attribution of the earthquake to the Hour is because it is one of its portents (أشراطها) and signs (أماراتها). It has also been said that what is meant by the earthquake of the Hour is its intensity and horror, though this is not without some linguistic distance.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يَوْمَ تَرَوْنَها تَذْهَلُ كُلُّ مُرْضِعَةٍ عَمَّا أَرْضَعَتْ** — **"The day you see it, every suckling mother will forget her infant..." (22:2)** Forgetfulness (الذهول) is to be distracted from something due to shock (دهشة). The term *haml* (الحمل) with a *fatha* on the first letter refers to a weight carried internally, like a fetus in the womb, while *himl* (الحمل) with a *kasra* refers to a weight carried externally, like the load of a camel; this was stated by al-Raghib. In *Majma‘ al-Bayan*, it is said: *haml* (الحمل) with a *fatha* is what is in a womb or on the head of a tree, and *himl* (الحمل) with a *kasra* is what is on a back or on a head.

It is stated in *al-Kashshaf*: "If it is asked: Why was it said *murdiah* (مُرْضِعَةٍ) [a woman actually suckling] rather than *murdi‘* [a woman who is in the period of lactation]? I say: The *murdiah* is the one who is in the very act of suckling, having her nipple in the child's mouth. The *murdi‘* is the one whose attribute is that she suckles, even if she is not engaged in the act at the moment she is described. Thus, *murdiah* was used to indicate that when she is suddenly overtaken by that horror while the infant has her nipple in its mouth, she will pull it out of its mouth due to the shock that afflicts her."

He also said: "If you ask: Why was it first said 'you [plural] see it' (ترونها) and then 'you [singular] see' (ترى) in the singular? I say: Because the vision was first attached to the earthquake, so all people were made observers of it. But finally, it is attached to the state of the people being drunk (سكرى), so each individual must be made an observer of the rest." [End of quote].

His saying: **وَتَرَى النَّاسَ سُكارى وَما هُمْ بِسُكارى** — **"...and you will see the people drunk, though they are not drunk..." (22:2)**. The negation of drunkenness after its affirmation serves to indicate that their "drunkenness"—which is the departure of their intellects and their falling into the depths of shock and bewilderment—is not caused by wine. Rather, it is the severity of Allah's punishment that has cast them into that state. As the Exalted has said: **إِنَّ أَخْذَهُ أَلِيمٌ شَدِيدٌ** — **"Indeed His seizing is painful and severe." (11:102)**.

The apparent meaning of the verse is that this earthquake occurs before the first blast (النفخة الأولى) of the trumpet, which the Exalted informs us of by saying: **وَنُفِخَ فِي الصُّورِ فَصَعِقَ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَمَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ إِلَّا مَنْ شاءَ اللهُ ثُمَّ نُفِخَ فِيهِ أُخْرى فَإِذا هُمْ قِيامٌ يَنْظُرُونَ** — **"And the Trumpet will be blown, and whoever is in the heavens and whoever is on the earth will swoon, except those whom Allah wishes. Then it will be blown another time, and behold, they will be standing, looking on." (39:68)**. This is because the verse assumes people are in a normal state when the earthquake of the Hour suddenly overtakes them, and their condition transforms upon witnessing it to what has been described. This must certainly be before the blast by which all living beings die.

It has been said that this is a metaphorical representation (تمثيل) of the severity of the punishment—meaning, if there were an observer there to see it, this would be the state. However, the occurrence of the verse in the context of warning and intimidation does not suit this interpretation well, as there is no point in warning about a punishment that cannot be perceived.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, it is narrated by Sa‘id ibn Mansur, Ahmad, ‘Abd ibn Humayd, al-Tirmidhi (who graded it authentic), al-Nasa’i, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Hakim (who graded it authentic), and Ibn Mardawayh through various chains from al-Hasan and others, from ‘Imran ibn Husayn, who said: "When the verses **يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ اتَّقُوا رَبَّكُمْ إِنَّ زَلْزَلَةَ السَّاعَةِ شَيْءٌ عَظِيمٌ** up to His saying **وَلكِنَّ عَذابَ اللهِ شَدِيدٌ** were revealed to him while he was on a journey, he said: 'Do you know what day that is?' They said: 'Allah and His Messenger know best.' He said: 'That is the day when Allah says to Adam: "Send forth the dispatch of the Fire." He will ask: "O Lord, what is the dispatch of the Fire?" He will reply: "From every thousand, nine hundred and ninety-nine to the Fire, and one to Paradise."'"

The Muslims began to weep, so the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "Be of good cheer and keep to the right course, for there has never been a prophethood except that it was preceded by a period of ignorance (جاهلية) — so the number is taken from that ignorance — if it is completed thereby, otherwise it is completed from the hypocrites. Your likeness is but like a mark on the foreleg of a beast, or like a mole on the side of a camel."

Then he said: "I hope that you will be a quarter of the inhabitants of Paradise." They cried out "Allahu Akbar." Then he said: "I hope that you will be a third of the inhabitants of Paradise." They cried out "Allahu Akbar." Then he said: "I hope that you will be half of the inhabitants of Paradise." They cried out "Allahu Akbar." The narrator said: "I do not know if he said 'two-thirds' or not."

I say: This is narrated through many other chains from ‘Imran, Ibn ‘Abbas, Abu Sa‘id al-Khudri, Abu Musa, and Anas, with variations in the texts; the most moderate of them is what we have cited.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding His saying: **وَتَرَى النَّاسَ سُكارى** — "and you will see the people drunk," he said: "Meaning their intellects have departed due to grief and terror, and they are bewildered."
